result:
# On Attention

I want to write about attention. Not the kind that corporations harvest and sell, not the kind that social media platforms engineer with infinite scrolls and dopamine loops, but the older, quieter kind—the attention that is essentially a form of love.

There is a particular quality of looking that happens when you truly see something. Last week I was walking through a parking lot and noticed a dandelion growing in the crack between the asphalt and the concrete curb. It was imperfect—one of its petals had been torn, probably by a passing tire, and the whole thing leaned at an angle that suggested it had been fighting wind from the delivery trucks that rumbled past each morning. But the yellow was so vivid against the gray that I stopped. I stood there for probably ten seconds, which is an unusually long time to stand in a parking lot looking at a weed, and I thought about how that dandelion had managed to root itself in essentially nothing, in the narrowest possible margin between hostile surfaces, and had still found whatever water and sunlight it needed to bloom. It was a small act of defiance against the entire built environment, which had been designed, with considerable expense and engineering, specifically to prevent anything like that dandelion from existing.

This is what attention does. It finds the thing that the world has tried to bury and brings it back to the surface. It refuses to accept the conclusion that the parking lot is only a parking lot. It insists, with the stubbornness of a child or a mystic, that there is more happening here than utility and commerce, that even in the most constructed and regulated spaces, life is performing its ancient experiments in form and color and persistence.

***

I think a lot about the relationship between attention and democracy. This might seem like a strange connection, but I believe it is one of the most important ones we have. Democracy depends on citizens being able to see each other clearly—not as categories or abstractions, not as data points in a polling algorithm, but as actual people with complicated lives and specific needs and unrepeatable histories. It depends on our willingness to attend to each other's reality, even when that reality is uncomfortable or inconvenient or runs against our preferred narratives.

The current crisis of democracy is, in many ways, a crisis of attention. We live in an information environment that actively discourages sustained focus on any single subject. The news cycle accelerates. The feed refreshes. The trending topics shift every few hours. We are trained to react rather than to consider, to scroll rather than to sit still. And this training has consequences for how we understand each other and how we make decisions together. When we cannot hold a thought long enough to trace its implications, when we cannot look at a person long enough to recognize their complexity, we become susceptible to the simplest and most dangerous political narratives: the ones that reduce human beings to types, the ones that turn disagreement into warfare, the ones that demand loyalty rather than understanding.

Attention is the antidote to this. Not the casual glance, not the reflexive judgment, but the deliberate practice of looking longer, asking more, and refusing to settle for the first interpretation that arrives. This kind of attention is not passive. It is an active and sometimes exhausting effort. It requires us to hold ourselves still when the world is urging us to move on, to remain present when distraction is offering itself as relief, to stay curious when certainty feels so much safer.

***

My grandmother kept a garden. It was not a large garden—it occupied a narrow strip of land between her house and the fence that marked the border of her property—but it was, in its way, a masterpiece. She grew tomatoes, beans, cucumbers, zucchini, herbs, and a row of sunflowers that by late August stood higher than the garage roof. She spent hours out there every day during the growing season, and I spent hours watching her, because even as a child I could sense that something important was happening when she worked in that dirt. She was paying attention. Not the scattered, fidgeting attention of someone looking for distraction, but the deep, absorbed attention of someone engaged in a conversation with the material world.

She would kneel in the soil and examine the leaves of each plant individually, turning them over with her fingers to check for insects or disease. She would feel the earth to judge its moisture. She would watch the sky for signs of changing weather. She knew the habits of the birds that visited the garden and the schedules of the insects that threatened it. She had, over decades of practice, developed a relationship with that small patch of ground that was intimate and responsive and fundamentally reciprocal. She attended to it, and in return it attended to her, feeding her not only with vegetables but with a sense of belonging and competence that I think she drew on for the rest of her life.

When she died, the garden went wild within a single season. The structures she had built—the trellises, the stakes, the careful borders—collapsed. The plants she had tended were overtaken by weeds and grass. Within two years, the plot was indistinguishable from the abandoned lots beside it. This was not a failure of the land. The land was doing what land does when attention withdraws. It was reverting to its own processes, its own logic, its own order. My grandmother's garden was not natural. It was the result of a sustained human intervention, a daily act of care that had shaped the ecosystem into a form that served human needs and desires. Without that care, the garden ceased to exist, because a garden is not a place. It is a practice.

I think about this often. What is a garden? It is not simply a collection of plants. It is not even simply a collection of plants arranged according to a particular design. It is a relationship between a person and a piece of land, maintained over time through continuous attention. The land has its own tendencies—it wants to move toward certain states, certain configurations of growth—and the gardener works with those tendencies, directing them, redirecting them, sometimes opposing them, always in dialogue with them. The result is something that neither the land nor the gardener could produce alone. It is a joint creation, an emergent phenomenon, and it exists only as long as the relationship exists. When the relationship ends, the garden ends, and what remains is just land again, doing what land does.

This is true of many things we value. A friendship is not a static entity. It is a continuous practice of attention. A community is not a geographic area or a demographic category. It is a network of sustained relationships, each maintained by ongoing acts of recognition and care. A skill is not a possession. It is a habit of engagement that must be exercised regularly or it decays. Even our sense of ourselves—our identity, our character, our understanding of who we are—is not something we acquire and hold. It is something we construct continuously through the way we attend to the world and to our own experience within it.

***

There is a passage in Simone Weil's writing that has stayed with me for years. She says: "Attention is the rarest and purest form of generosity." I have thought about this sentence many times, and each time I find more in it. At first glance, it seems straightforward: to pay attention to someone is to give them something of value, something that cannot be bought or fabricated, something that requires your time and your presence and your willingness to see them as they actually are rather than as you prefer them to be. This is already a profound idea. In a culture that constantly pushes us toward distraction and superficial engagement, the simple act of actually looking at another person—listening to them, noticing them, taking them seriously—becomes a kind of gift, even a kind of resistance.

But Weil's sentence contains more than this. The word "rarest" suggests that attention is scarce, that it is difficult to produce, that there is something precious about it. And this is true. Attention requires energy. It requires us to set aside our own preoccupations and agendas and open ourselves to what is before us. It requires us to tolerate uncertainty, because when we truly attend to something, we do not yet know what we will find, and we must be willing to let the object of our attention reveal itself on its own terms rather than conforming to our expectations. This is hard. It is much easier to glance and categorize, to project and assume, to move on. Full attention demands a kind of surrender—a willingness to be changed by what we see.

And the word "purest" suggests that attention has a quality that other forms of generosity lack. When we give money or time or advice, we are often giving from a position of surplus. We are distributing something we have in abundance. But attention is different. We cannot give attention without also giving ourselves. The act of attending requires our full presence; it cannot be delegated or automated or scaled. This makes it inherently personal, inherently embodied, inherently limited. And this limitation is what makes it pure. There is no way to fake attention. There is no way to give it without actually giving it. It cannot be diluted or hedged or offered conditionally. It is either whole or it is not attention at all.

This purity is what makes attention so valuable and so threatening. When someone truly attends to us, they see things we may have hidden or denied or simply not noticed. They reflect us back to ourselves in a way that is accurate and unflinching, and this can be uncomfortable. It can also be transformative. To be seen clearly, to be recognized as a particular and specific person rather than as a representative of a category, is one of the deepest human needs. It is the basis of genuine connection. And it depends entirely on the quality of attention we receive.

***

I have been thinking about what it means to pay attention to something that cannot respond. A painting, for instance. A landscape. A stone wall in the woods that was built by someone a hundred years ago and has been slowly dissolving back into the earth ever since. When we attend to these things, we are not engaged in a dialogue in the ordinary sense. They do not answer us. They do not acknowledge our presence or return our interest. And yet something happens when we look at them carefully, when we let them occupy our awareness fully. We begin to notice details that were invisible to our casual glance. We begin to see patterns and relationships that were hidden. We begin, in a strange way, to hear something—to receive something from the object that feels like communication, even though the object is not, in any conventional sense, communicating.

I think this is what the artists and the naturalists and the mystics are describing when they talk about contemplation. It is not a passive state. It is an active engagement with the world that proceeds without words, without categories, without the constant intermediary of analytical thought. It is a form of knowing that relies on direct encounter rather than abstraction. And it produces a different kind of understanding than analysis produces—one that is more intimate, more particular, more rooted in the specific reality of the thing observed rather than in general principles derived from many observations.

This kind of attention is difficult to sustain. Our minds are built to process, to categorize, to move on. They are built to extract useful information from the environment and then redirect our focus to the next stimulus. Staying with something—remaining present with it long after our initial curiosity has been satisfied, long after we have formed our first interpretation—requires a kind of discipline that runs against our cognitive instincts. It requires what the psychologist Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi called "flow," but it also requires something beyond flow. It requires a willingness to be bored, to be confused, to be uncertain, to stay in a state of not-knowing long enough for a deeper knowing to emerge.

I have experienced this most clearly in my own practice of writing. When I first approach a subject, I have a set of initial ideas about it—things I've read, associations I've formed, arguments I've encountered. These ideas are useful. They give me a starting point. But they are also limiting. They represent the easiest path through the material, the most obvious route from premise to conclusion. If I follow them without resistance, I will produce something competent but predictable. The writing will confirm what I already believe. It will not surprise me. It will not teach me anything I did not already know.

The alternative is to slow down. To resist the first interpretation. To sit with the material longer—to read the sources again, to look at the evidence again, to ask the same questions again—not because I expect different answers, but because I expect deeper ones. I want to reach the point where my initial framework begins to crack, where my comfortable assumptions start to feel insufficient, where the subject itself begins to push back against my understanding of it and demand a more adequate response. This is uncomfortable. It feels like failure. It feels like I have lost my grasp on the material. But it is actually the beginning of real engagement. It is the moment when the subject starts to teach me rather than me simply using the subject to confirm what I already think.

Every good piece of writing I have produced has involved this process. Every piece of writing that actually taught me something went through a phase where I felt lost, where the structure I had imagined collapsed, where the argument I had planned stopped working, where I had to abandon my outline and start over from a different angle. And in every case, the breakthrough came not from thinking harder about the problem but from paying closer attention to the material. From reading the text again. From looking at the evidence again. From noticing something I had overlooked the first or fifth or tenth time through. Attention, in this context, is not a preliminary step. It is the entire method.

***

We are living through a time when the value of attention is being systematically eroded. The attention economy—the vast complex of platforms, algorithms, and design principles that has been constructed to capture and monetize our awareness—is not simply competing for our time. It is reshaping our cognitive habits. It is training us to prefer brief encounters over sustained engagement, to value novelty over depth, to respond to stimulation rather than to seek understanding. It is altering, at a fundamental level, how we relate to the world and to each other.

This is not a conspiracy. It is not something that was planned by a cabal of evil technologists. It is the logical outcome of a set of economic incentives that reward engagement above all else—where engagement is measured in clicks, views, time-on-page, and shares, and where the content that produces these metrics most efficiently is content that provokes strong emotional reactions quickly. The system does not care whether those reactions are positive or negative. It does not care whether the content is true or false. It cares only whether it captures attention. And the most reliable way to capture attention is to bypass the slower, more deliberate processes of judgment and consideration and go straight to the reflexive, the emotional, the reactive.

Against this, the practice of attention appears almost quixotic. It is slow. It is inefficient. It produces no measurable metrics. It cannot be optimized or scaled. It resists the logic of the market. And yet it remains one of the most valuable things we can do—not just for ourselves, but for the world. Because attention is how we discover what is actually happening. It is how we learn what matters and what doesn't. It is how we build the knowledge and understanding that allows us to act wisely. It is how we connect with each other as particular, irreplaceable people rather than as data points in an algorithm's model. It is how we maintain the capacity for wonder, for curiosity, for the kind of encounter that changes us.

The question, then, is not whether attention is valuable. It clearly is. The question is how to protect and cultivate it in an environment that is constantly attacking it. And I think the answer begins with recognizing that attention is not simply a cognitive resource. It is not something we have in a fixed quantity and then allocate according to demand. It is a practice—a habit, a discipline, a way of relating to the world that can be developed or lost, strengthened or weakened. Like any practice, it requires regular exercise. It requires us to set aside time and space where the noise of the attention economy cannot reach us, where we can look at something without being prompted to look away, where we can follow our own curiosity rather than being directed by an algorithm's prediction of what we will find engaging.

This does not require withdrawal from the world. It does not require monastic seclusion or the abandonment of technology. It requires something more difficult and more interesting: the development of a dual capacity. We need to be able to engage with the fast, noisy, stimulating world of digital information without losing our ability to engage with the slow, quiet, demanding world of sustained attention. We need to be able to scroll and also to sit still. We need to be able to react and also to consider. We need to be able to connect widely and also to know deeply. These are not opposites. They are complements. And a life that includes both is richer than a life that includes only one.

***

I want to end by returning to that dandelion in the parking lot. When I saw it, I was on my way somewhere—I had a destination, a schedule, a purpose. The dandelion was not part of any of those things. It was an interruption, a deviation, a moment when my planned trajectory through the day was altered by something I had not anticipated and did not know how to categorize. And yet that moment of interruption was also a moment of encounter. The dandelion became, for those ten seconds, the most important thing in my world. It demanded nothing from me. It offered nothing to me. It simply existed, in that crack between the asphalt and the curb, doing what dandelions do: growing, blooming, being yellow, being alive.

There is a kind of freedom in this kind of attention. Not freedom from responsibility or freedom from purpose, but freedom from the tyranny of the planned and the expected. Freedom to be surprised. Freedom to notice what the schedule did not leave room for. Freedom to find value in what was not valued by the systems that structure our days. This is not an escape from the world. It is a more honest way of being in it. Because the world, in its actual unfolding, is always more various and more surprising than any plan can contain. And our lives, in their actual living, are always richer than any metric can measure.

Attention is the practice that makes this richness available. It is the act of looking that finds the dandelion in the parking lot. It is the act of listening that hears the particular voice inside the general noise. It is the act of staying present that lets the moment reveal itself fully. It is, as Simone Weil said, the rarest and purest form of generosity. And it is, I believe, one of the most important things we can offer each other and offer the world—not just now, in a time of distraction and crisis, but always, in every time, in every place, in every life that is trying to find its way toward meaning.

I want to end by returning to that dandelion in the parking lot. When I saw it, I was on my way somewhere—I had a destination, a schedule, a purpose. The dandelion was not part of any of those things. It was an interruption, a deviation, a moment when my planned trajectory through the day was altered by something I had not anticipated and did not know how to categorize. And yet that moment of interruption was also a moment of encounter. The dandelion became, for those ten seconds, the most important thing in my world. It demanded nothing from me. It offered nothing to me. It simply existed, in that crack between the asphalt and the curb, doing what dandelions do: growing, blooming, being yellow, being alive.

There is a kind of freedom in this kind of attention. Not freedom from responsibility or freedom from purpose, but freedom from the tyranny of the planned and the expected. Freedom to be surprised. Freedom to notice what the schedule did not leave room for. Freedom to find value in what was not valued by the systems that structure our days. This is not an escape from the world. It is a more honest way of being in it. Because the world, in its actual unfolding, is always more various and more surprising than any plan can contain. And our lives, in their actual living, are always richer than any metric can measure.

Attention is the practice that makes this richness available. It is the act of looking that finds the dandelion in the parking lot. It is the act of listening that hears the particular voice inside the general noise. It is the act of staying present that lets the moment reveal itself fully. It is, as Simone Weil said, the rarest and purest form of generosity. And it is, I believe, one of the most important things we can offer each other and offer the world—not just now, in a time of distraction and crisis, but always, in every time, in every place, in every life that is trying to find its way toward meaning.

I think I'll write about the concept of attention - how we direct it, what it means, how it shapes our reality. It's a topic that has philosophical depth, practical relevance, and allows for personal reflection. I can weave in observations about modern life, ancient wisdom, neuroscience, and personal experience.

Let me write something that feels genuinely thoughtful and engaged, not formulaic. I'll aim for prose that has rhythm and texture.

Let me write about attention and presence, the modern condition, and what it means to truly see something.
